title: Trauma and the Wholeness System: Reclaiming All Parts of Yourself After Trauma
excerpt: Explore how trauma fragments the self and discover science-backed ways to reclaim your wholeness through somatic healing and nervous system regulation.
body:
Understanding Trauma as a Fragmentation of the Wholeness System

Trauma isn’t just an event in the past; it’s a disruption in the entire system that makes up who you are. When Bessel van der Kolk describes trauma, he highlights how it changes the brain and body’s ability to feel safe and integrated. This creates a fracturing of your internal experience, where different parts of yourself become disconnected or stuck in survival mode. Trauma fragments your wholeness — the cohesive sense of self that includes your thoughts, emotions, bodily sensations, and memories.

According to Richard Schwartz’s Internal Family Systems (IFS) model, which is grounded in decades of clinical research, these fragmented parts are not pathological but protective. They arise to manage unbearable feelings and experiences. Yet, when these parts are left unacknowledged or suppressed, they generate internal conflict and keep you stuck in trauma responses long after the event itself has passed.

What Is the Wholeness System?

The wholeness system can be thought of as the integrated network of your mind, body, and nervous system functioning in harmony. Dan Siegel refers to this as "mindsight" — the capacity to hold all aspects of yourself in awareness without judgment. It includes:

  • Your cognitive self: thoughts, beliefs, and narratives.
  • Your emotional self: feelings and affective states.
  • Your somatic self: sensations and bodily experiences.
  • Your relational self: how you connect with others.

Trauma disrupts this system, often leaving parts of you isolated and unable to communicate with one another. This disintegration perpetuates symptoms such as hypervigilance, numbness, and emotional overwhelm.

How Trauma Affects the Nervous System and Body-Based Selfhood

Stephen Porges’s Polyvagal Theory offers a crucial framework for understanding trauma’s impact on the nervous system. The autonomic nervous system (ANS) isn’t just about fight or flight; it’s a hierarchical system that also includes a social engagement pathway and a shutdown (freeze) mode.

When trauma hits, your nervous system can become locked in defensive states, impairing your ability to access the full range of your internal resources. This dysregulation manifests as bodily symptoms like chronic tension, dissociation, or difficulty grounding in the present moment. Peter Levine, founder of Somatic Experiencing, emphasizes that trauma is stored in the body and must be resolved through bodily awareness and release rather than cognitive processing alone.

"Trauma is not what happens to you, but what happens inside you as a result of what happens to you." — Gabor Maté

The Role of Somatic Awareness in Reclaiming Wholeness

Somatic awareness is the practice of tuning into bodily sensations and signals. It taps into the pre-verbal, implicit memory stored in the nervous system. Francine Shapiro’s work in EMDR therapy also shows how bilateral stimulation can help the brain process traumatic memories that are stuck in frozen, fragmented states.

By cultivating somatic awareness, you begin to re-establish communication between fragmented parts of yourself. This process activates the ventral vagal complex in Porges’s model, supporting feelings of safety and connection. Deb Dana, a leading clinician in Polyvagal-informed therapy, stresses the importance of learning to recognize and shift autonomic states to regain regulation and integration.

Internal Family Systems: Embracing and Healing Your Parts

Internal Family Systems (IFS), developed by Richard Schwartz, provides a practical roadmap for working with the multiplicity of the self. IFS recognizes that every person has multiple parts, including exiles (wounded parts), managers (protective parts), and firefighters (reactive parts). These parts often hold traumatic memories or emotions that the conscious self can’t fully access.

Rather than trying to eliminate these parts, IFS encourages you to build a relationship with them from a place of curiosity and compassion. This process fosters internal dialogue and integration, helping you reclaim the wholeness lost after trauma. Pat Ogden’s Sensorimotor Psychotherapy complements IFS by emphasizing the importance of bodily tracking and movement in freeing stuck trauma parts.

Steps to Reclaim Your Wholeness System

  • Recognize and name your parts: Begin by identifying different emotional or bodily states without judgment.
  • Develop somatic awareness: Practice mindful attention to bodily sensations and autonomic cues.
  • Use nervous system regulation techniques: Engage in practices like deep breathing, grounding, or safe social connection to activate the ventral vagal complex.
  • Build compassionate internal dialogue: Approach your protective and wounded parts with curiosity and empathy.
  • Integrate through movement and expression: Somatic therapies, expressive arts, or gentle movement can support integration.

The Science Behind Integration: Why Wholeness Matters

Integration is a core concept in trauma recovery. Dan Siegel defines integration as linking differentiated parts of the self into a coherent whole. Neuroscience shows that trauma disrupts the connections between brain regions responsible for emotion regulation, memory, and executive function. When these connections are restored, individuals experience improved resilience and well-being.

Research into neuroplasticity confirms that the brain can rewire itself through intentional practices. This means that reclaiming your wholeness system isn’t just hopeful—it’s biologically plausible. The combined work of van der Kolk, Levine, and others underscores that healing trauma requires addressing the body, mind, and relational contexts simultaneously.

Beyond Therapy: How to Continue Reclaiming Your Wholeness on Your Own

If you’ve tried talk therapy and still feel stuck, somatic healing offers a path forward that honors your lived experience and body intelligence. Here are practical ways to take ownership of your healing journey:

  • Practice Body Scanning: Daily check-ins with your body can help identify where trauma is held.
  • Engage in Safe Movement: Yoga, Tai Chi, or gentle stretching can help release tension and promote integration.
  • Use Grounding Techniques: Focus on sensory details around you to stay present when overwhelmed.
  • Develop a Supportive Inner Relationship: Use journaling or visualization to dialogue with your parts.
  • Seek Trauma-Informed Communities: Connection with others who understand trauma validation supports nervous system regulation and belonging.
"Healing trauma is not about becoming someone new, but reclaiming the parts of yourself that were lost or hidden."

When to Seek Professional Somatic Support

While self-directed work is powerful, trauma can sometimes require skilled guidance. Therapists trained in Somatic Experiencing, Sensorimotor Psychotherapy, EMDR, or IFS can help you navigate intense emotions and stuck somatic patterns safely. These modalities all emphasize body-based processing and relational safety as keys to reclaiming your wholeness.

Remember, the goal isn’t to erase trauma but to transform your relationship with it, restoring balance and internal harmony. As Gabor Maté reminds us, trauma recovery is a deep, ongoing process of compassion for all parts of yourself.

Conclusion

Trauma fractures the wholeness system that integrates your mind, body, and emotions. However, science-backed approaches—from Polyvagal Theory to Internal Family Systems—show us that reclaiming all parts of yourself is not only possible but essential. Through somatic awareness, nervous system regulation, and compassionate internal dialogue, you can reconnect fragmented parts and restore your internal harmony. Healing trauma is a journey of reclaiming your full self, piece by piece.

By understanding the biological and psychological effects of trauma, and committing to body-based healing practices, you empower yourself to move beyond survival and toward thriving.

faqs.q: What does it mean to have fragmented parts after trauma?
faqs.a: Trauma can cause parts of your internal experience—like emotions, memories, or sensations—to become disconnected or isolated. These 'parts' often serve protective roles but may create inner conflict if left unintegrated.

faqs.q: How does somatic healing help with trauma recovery?
faqs.a: Somatic healing focuses on bodily awareness and nervous system regulation, helping to release stored trauma and restore safety signals in the body, which supports reintegration of fragmented self-parts.

faqs.q: Can I reclaim my wholeness system without a therapist?
faqs.a: Yes, self-directed somatic practices like body scanning, grounding, and compassionate inner dialogue can be effective. However, professional support can be valuable for navigating intense trauma safely.

faqs.q: What are some reliable trauma healing modalities based on this research?
faqs.a: Internal Family Systems (IFS), Somatic Experiencing, Sensorimotor Psychotherapy, EMDR, and Polyvagal-informed therapies are all research-backed approaches that address trauma through body and mind integration.

affiliateProducts.title: The Body Keeps the Score by Bessel van der Kolk
affiliateProducts.description: A foundational book explaining how trauma impacts the body and brain, offering science-based approaches to healing through somatic awareness.

metaTitle: Trauma & Wholeness: Reclaiming Yourself After Trauma
metaDescription: Learn how trauma fragments your self and discover somatic healing methods to reclaim your wholeness and nervous system regulation.
author: The Oracle Lover
